attractions.name: Broken Ground Conservation Area (Broken Ground Trails)
attractions.description: The Broken Ground Conservation Area features a five-square-mile expanse of diverse physical features, including forests, wetlands, and glacial boulders. The area remained largely undeveloped since 1725 and now offers over five miles of hiking and mountain biking trails that meander through historical homestead remains and environmentally sensitive beaver ponds. (Concord, NH Document Center)
attractions.address: South Curtisville Rd or Portsmouth St trailhead parking
attractions.hours: Open daily from dawn to dusk (Concord, NH)
attractions.admission: Free (Concord, NH)
highlights: 'Over 5 miles of multi-use trails for hiking and mountain biking ([Concord, NH)', 'Historical remains of the Edmond Curtis and Benjamin Kimball homesteads (Trail Finder)', 'Scenic overlooks of active beaver ponds and diverse wetlands (Concord, NH Document Center)', 'Glacial boulders and unique topography left by retreating glaciers (Trail Finder)', 'Abundant wildlife viewing opportunities including great blue herons and beavers (Concord, NH Document Center)', 'Interconnected loops like the Marsh Loop and Howard C. Nowell Trail (Komoot)']
attractions.tips: Keep dogs leashed, especially near sensitive beaver ponds, and stay on marked trails to protect the natural habitat. Be prepared for country hiking conditions as some trails can be long or rough. (Concord, NH)

attractions.name: Downtown Concord Historic District
attractions.description: The Downtown Concord Historic District encompasses the commercial heart of New Hampshire's capital, featuring a remarkably well-preserved collection of 19th-century architecture. Centered on North and South Main Streets, the district serves as a vibrant hub for shopping, dining, and government activity, reflecting the city's growth from a 1734 incorporation to its status as a state capital. Visitors can explore a pedestrian-friendly environment that highlights the city's rich history through landmarks like the Merrimack County Bank building and the iconic Eagle Hotel.

attractions.tips: Explore the district on foot to best appreciate the historical markers and architectural details. Download a self-guided walking tour map or use the New Hampshire Historical Society app to enhance your visit.

attractions.name: Eagle Hotel
attractions.description: The Eagle Hotel is a historic five-story brick landmark in downtown Concord, NH, originally built in 1851 on the site of the Eagle Coffee House. It served as a premier meeting place for U.S. Presidents, state politicians, and celebrities for over a century before closing in 1961 and being added to the National Register of Historic Places. Today, the building houses offices and retail shops while remaining a central piece of the city's architectural heritage.

attractions.name: New Hampshire State House

The capitol features a notable gold-leafed dome and granite exterior, serving as a prominent symbol of New Hampshire's government and history New Hampshire General Court.

attractions.name: The Pierce Manse
attractions.description: The Pierce Manse is the only home ever owned and lived in by Franklin Pierce, the 14th President of the United States. This Greek Revival house, which the Pierce family occupied from 1842 to 1848, was saved from demolition in 1971 and moved to its current location in Concord's historic district. Today, the museum offers guided tours that showcase 19th-century domestic life through period furnishings, personal artifacts, and primary source documents (Official Website).

attractions.name: Wadleigh State Park
attractions.description: Wadleigh State Park is a scenic recreation area located on the southeast shore of the 170-acre Kezar Lake in North Sutton, New Hampshire. The park is renowned for its tall pines that provide ample shade for picnic sites adjacent to a sandy beach, making it an ideal destination for families and quiet retreats. Visitors can enjoy swimming in crystal-clear waters, fishing, and boating, with opportunities to paddle to a small island or hike a three-mile loop around the lake.

attractions.tips: Parking is limited and can fill up quickly on nice weekends and holidays, so making a day-use reservation up to 30 days in advance is highly recommended. Note that pets are strictly not permitted in the park, and visitors should follow the Carry-In/Carry-Out policy for trash. Lifeguards are not typically on duty, so swimming is at your own risk. Pack a cooler as there is no snack bar on-site, though a general store is within walking distance.

attractions.name: White Park
attractions.description: White Park is a historic 25-acre public park established in 1884 through a land donation by Armenia White in memory of her husband. Designed by renowned architect Charles Elliott, the park features a picturesque man-made pond, mature shade trees, and rolling hills that serve as a year-round destination for recreation and community events. It is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and is home to one of Concord's most beloved playgrounds and its premier outdoor winter skating rink. (Concord NH Official Website, Wikipedia))

attractions.tips: Use the main parking lot at 1 White Street for direct access to the playground and lodge; no alcohol is permitted anywhere in the park or lodge facilities; visit during the autumn for some of the city's best fall foliage; check the Parks & Recreation Facebook page for the most current updates on seasonal facility openings and ice conditions. (Concord NH Official Website, White Mountain IT Services)

attractions.name: Marjory Swope Park (at Penacook Lake)
attractions.description: Marjory Swope Park is a 77-acre conservation area in West Concord that offers a scenic network of trails surrounding Penacook Lake, the city's primary water supply. Donated in 2012 as a memorial to a local conservationist, the park features diverse landscapes including pine forests, open meadows, and dramatic granite outcroppings. It serves as a gateway to a larger network of interconnected conserved lands, providing popular routes for hiking, mountain biking, and wildlife viewing. Visit Concord NH, Five Rivers Conservation Trust
attractions.hours: 6:00 AM - 11:00 PM Daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: ['Panoramic views of Penacook Lake from the Blue Loop trail vista points.', 'The summit of Jerry Hill, offering vistas of southwestern New Hampshire landmarks like Mount Monadnock.', 'Gilfillan Rock, a massive and historically significant granite outcropping.', 'Historical foundation of a former 360-degree observation tower on the summit trail.', 'Interconnected trail system linking to Winant Park and Carter Hill Orchard.']
attractions.tips: The Blue Loop is the most popular for lake views; use the Yellow or Orange trails for a steeper climb to the summit. The park is pet-friendly but dogs must remain leashed. Parking is available at the trailhead on Long Pond Road, approximately 0.75 miles from Hopkinton Road. City of Concord Trail Guide

attractions.name: Prescott Park

Established in the 1930s through the bequest of the Prescott sisters, the park features meticulously maintained formal gardens, public docks, and historical structures. It serves as a major cultural hub, most notably hosting the Prescott Park Arts Festival which brings outdoor theater, concerts, and family events to the community each summer. Wikipedia), City of Portsmouth

attractions.name: Rollins Park
attractions.description: Established in 1892 through a land donation by Governor Frank West Rollins, this 34-acre park is a cornerstone of the South End community. It features the city's largest public pool, a destination playground, and a historic stone bridge and gazebo that are popular for weddings. The park serves as a year-round hub for recreation, offering everything from summer swimming and sports to winter ice skating. (City of Concord, Airial Travel)

The gazebo and bridge area is a beautiful spot for photos, especially during wedding season. During the winter, check the local recreation calendar for the opening of the seasonal ice rink. (City of Concord, Airial Travel)

attractions.name: South End, Concord, NH
attractions.description: The South End is a historic and well-established residential neighborhood in Concord, New Hampshire, characterized by a mix of mid-20th-century homes and historic Victorian architecture. It offers a peaceful, walkable suburban atmosphere while providing quick access to downtown amenities and the Interstate 93 corridor. The neighborhood is particularly favored by families and commuters for its proximity to parks, schools, and major transportation routes.

attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (Neighborhood); Rollins Park typically dawn to dusk; Rollins Pool seasonal summer hours (approx. 10 am - 7 pm).
attractions.admission: Free (Neighborhood and Rollins Park); Fees apply for non-resident pool use ($25/family for 48-hour pass)
highlights: ['Rollins Park: A historic 1892 park featuring a public pool, tennis courts, and walking paths.', 'Concord Gas Light Company Gasholder House: A rare, intact 1888 industrial landmark on the National Register of Historic Places.', 'Memorial Field: A major community hub for youth sports and neighborhood outdoor recreation.', 'Historic Architecture: Tree-lined streets showcasing beautifully preserved Victorian, Colonial, and Craftsman-style homes.', 'Walkable Access: Convenient walking distance to downtown Concord’s shops, restaurants, and the State House.', 'I-93 Proximity: Excellent positioning for easy commuting to Manchester or Boston.']

It is a quiet residential area, so be mindful of local traffic and parking regulations.

It features over 30 historic buildings, heirloom gardens, and costumed roleplayers that bring Portsmouth's history alive from the 17th century to the mid-20th century. Strawbery Banke Official Site

It is one of the few breweries in the state that is dog-friendly both indoors and outdoors year-round (Henniker Brewing Official Website).

attractions.description: The Merrimack River Greenway Trail (MRGT) is a premier multi-use paved trail system in Concord, New Hampshire, designed for walking, cycling, and non-motorized recreation. Following the scenic banks of the Merrimack River, the trail connects local neighborhoods and open spaces while promoting health and environmental stewardship. When completed, it will span 12.7 miles, linking the Northern Rail Trail in Boscawen to the future Pembroke Rail Trail.

attractions.tips: The trail is not maintained during winter months and can be icy; proceed with caution. Best access is found at the Gully Hill Fields trailhead or Terrill Park. Dogs are welcome on leashes, and the Terrill Park section includes a dedicated off-leash dog park. MRGT

attractions.name: Merrimack River Outdoor Education & Conservation Area (MROECA)
attractions.description: The Merrimack River Outdoor Education & Conservation Area, often called 'The Floodplain,' is a 103-acre sanctuary featuring a diverse landscape of oxbows, back channels, and high terraces. It hosts a unique silver maple floodplain forest and provides a vital habitat for wildlife including bald eagles, turtles, and migratory birds. Visitors can explore the area via the 1.5-mile Les Clark Nature Trail, which offers scenic views and educational interpretations of the river's dynamic ecosystem (Forest Society).

attractions.hours: Dawn to Dusk (Monday-Sunday) (Forest Society)
attractions.admission: Free (Forest Society)

attractions.tips: Stay off the steep river banks to protect nesting bank swallows and prevent erosion; keep pets under active vocal control; be aware that the parking gate is locked at dusk and towing is enforced (Forest Society).

The cemetery features beautifully maintained grounds, a Memorial Walkway, and historical monuments that commemorate the state's rich military heritage (NHSVC Introduction).

attractions.hours: Dawn to dusk
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: ['Scenic views of Glen Lake and the Piscataquog River', 'Connection to the Piscataquog Trail leading into Manchester', "Historical landmarks including a potter's field and old rail storage buildings", "Passes through the 'Rock Cut' where cliffs were blasted for the railway", 'Direct access to Glen Lake for swimming and fishing', 'Level gravel surface suitable for walking, running, and hybrid biking']
attractions.tips: The trail surface is mostly hard-packed gravel, so hybrid or mountain bikes are recommended over road bikes. Be cautious at street crossings, particularly over Mast Road (Route 114). Restrooms are available at the Parks and Recreation Center when the building is open. Pet owners must keep animals leashed and clean up after them. Use the interactive online maps to find the various parking access points such as Factory Street or Sarette Field.
